title: Electroplating of cobalt from aqueous citrate baths
abstract:
J. Chem. T echnol. Biotechnol. 1998, 73, 369È376
Electroplating of Cobalt from Aqueous Citrate
Baths
Sayed S. Abd El Rehim,* Saad M. Abd El Wahaab, Magdy A. M. Ibrahim &
Marwan M. Dankeria
Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Ain Shams University, Cairo, Egypt
(Received 17 October 1997; revised version received 1 April 1998; accepted 12 August 1998)
Abstract: Electroplating of cobalt onto steel substrates from citrate baths has
been investigated under di†erent conditions of bath composition, current density,
pH and temperature. A detailed study has been made of the inÑuence of these
variables on the potentiodynamic cathodic polarization curves, cathodic current
efficiency and the throwing power as well as the throwing index of these baths.
The
optimum
bath
composition
has
been
established
and
it
contains:
(0É36 mol dm~3) trisodium citrate (0É19 mol dm~3) and citric
CoSO4 . 7H2O
acid (0É1 mol dm~3) at pH 5É0. The microhardness of cobalt electrodeposited
from citrate baths is high and it may be, under certain conditions, two or three
times higher than that reported for cobalt electrodeposited from other di†erent
baths. The surface morphology of the as-plated cobalt was investigated by using
scanning electron microscopy (SEM) while the structure was studied by using
X-ray di†raction analysis and anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) techniques.

Key words: electroplating; cobalt; citrate baths
INTRODUCTION
Electroplating of cobalt and its alloys is used for the
purpose of corrosion protection.1 Cobalt, and especially
porous cobalt produced by reactive deposition, are also
very active in the evolution of oxygen and hydrogen
from alkaline solutions.2,3 Moreover, the magnetic
properties of cobalt and its alloys are of especial interest
in electronic applications, particularly in the computer
industry.3,4
The electrodeposition of cobalt from aqueous solu-
tions has received much less attention by comparison
with metals such as copper or nickel.5h8 However,
attempts have been made to electrodeposit cobalt from
di†erent baths.9h16 The e†ect of solution pH on the
structure and magnetic properties of cobalt Ðlms elec-
trodeposited from simple
and
solutions
CoSO4
CoCl2
has been reported.17,18
The objective of the present study is to develop baths
from which cobalt metal could be electrodeposited from
* To whom correspondence should be addressed.
citrate solutions. The advantages of these baths are not
only their cheapness but also their non-polluting e†ect
on the environment. In this paper, the inÑuence of the
citrate bath constituents and some operating variables
on the characteristics of both the electroplating process
and the resulting cobalt electrodeposit were studied.
EXPERIMENTAL
All the plating baths and reagents used were made from
Analar chemicals and doubly distilled water. The com-
positions of the baths examined for cobalt electrodepo-
sition are given in Table 1. For electrodeposition, a steel
cathode and platinum sheet anode, both of dimensions
2É5 cm ] 3É0 cm, were used. Platinum sheet was sur-
rounded by an asbestos diaphragm to preclude oxida-
tion of Co2` ions and errors in the measured potentials
caused by oxygen evolved at the anode. The plating cell
used was a rectangular Perspex trough (10 cm ] 3 cm)
provided with vertical grooves, on each of the side
walls, to Ðx the electrodes. Before each run, the steel

Potentiodynamic cathodic polarization curves
The data on Fig. 1 reveal that the cobalt deposited from
a bath containing only cobalt sulphate solutions (0É21È
Fig. 3. Polarization curves during Co-electrodeposition from
solution bath 10 at di†erent temperatures (curve a T \ 25¡C,
curve b T \ 35¡C and curve c T \ 50¡C).
0É36 mol dm~3) (curves aÈc) is characterized by a slight
polarization mainly due to activation polarization.
Moreover, increasing the Co2` content in the bath
slightly shifts the deposition potential towards the less
negative values. These results could be attributed to
increases in the relative concentration of Co2`, particu-
larly in the cathode di†usion layer, and this is reÑected
in decreases in concentration polarization associated
with cobalt deposition. Deposition under these condi-
However, preliminary experiments showed that smooth,
bright and adherent deposits could be obtained in the
presence of a mixture of citric and trisodium citrate.
Inspection of the data of Fig. 1 reveals that the pres-
ence of either trisodium citrate (curves d and e) or citric
acid (curves f and g) in the sulphate solution shifts
remarkably the cathodic polarization to more negative
values. The shift in polarization could be attributed to
the formation of CoÈcitrate complexes.21 According to
the literature,21,22 in a bath containing CoSO4 . 7H2O
(0É36 mol dm~3) trisodium citrate (0É19 mol dm~3) and
occurred (the equilibrium constant K for each is given):
K(Co2` ] H2Cit~ \ CoH2Cit`) 0É18 ] 102
CoCit~ is the predominant complex species in the solu-
tion rather than other complex species because it has a
much higher equilibrium constant. In addition, since
there are more Co2` ions than Cit. ions, there will also
be signiÐcant amounts of free Co2` present in the solu-
tion at all pH values. Therefore, the following electro-
evolution from independent side reactions:
The increase in cathodic polarization observed in the
presence of citrate is due to the formation of CoCit~
which cannot be reduced so easily as the free Co2`. It is
worthy of attention that an increase in concentration of
trisodium citrate increases the cathodic polarization,
while the increasing cathodic polarization e†ect of citric
acid diminishes with increases in the acid concentration.
These contradictory results could be explained on the
basis of pH changes. Increasing the concentration of tri-
sodium citrate increases the pH of the bath while
increasing the concentration of the acid decreases its
pH. Therefore, some experiments were performed in
0É19 mol dm~3 trisodium citrate but of di†erent pH
values (the pH of the baths was changed by NaOH
S. S. Abd El Rehim, S. M. Abd El W ahaab, M. A. M. Ibrahim, M. M. Dankeria
addition). From the results shown in Fig. 2, it is clear
that at constant ratios of citric/citrate in the bath, the
cathodic polarization increases as the pH increases. The
e†ect of pH on cathodic polarization can be understood
by examining the relationship between reacting species
and pH. Varying the pH while keeping the concentra-
tion of the metal salt and citric/citrate ratio in the bath
constant, changes the ratio of CoCit~ to Co2`. Lower
pH values increase the concentration of Co2` ions,
leading to a reduction in cathodic polarization.
The inÑuence of temperature on the cathodic polar-
ization during cobalt electroplating from bath 10 was
examined and the results are given in Fig. 3. A rise of
bath temperature shifts the cathodic polarization curves
to less negative values. This behaviour could be attrib-
uted to a decrease in the activation polarization of the
reducible species.23 Moreover, an increase of tem-
perature enhances the concentration of the reducible
species in the di†usion layer as a result of increasing the
di†usion rates. In addition, the e†ect of temperature
on the relative abundance of both the complexed and
uncomplexed species in the solution is considered.
The e†ect of both Co content as well as citric/citrate
ratio in the bath on the cathodic current efficiency, /,
during cobalt electrodeposition was studied and the
results are shown in Fig. 4. The data indicate that the %
/ at a given pH (pH \ 4É0) depends strongly on both
citric/citrate on the cathodic current efficiency at constant
pH \ 4É0; curve a, citric/citrate ratio \ 0É5; curve b, citric/
citrate ratio \ 2É0; i \ 2 A dm~2.
the citric/citrate ratio and Co content in the bath. At a
given Co content in the bath, the % / is found to
decrease with increases in the citric/citrate ratio. It is
possible that an increase in the citric/citrate ratio at a
constant pH value lowers the Co2` content in the bath.
Therefore, one expected a decrease in cathodic current
efficiency in this case. This explanation can be sup-
ported from the observation that an increase in the
metal salt concentration, i.e. the concentration of the
free Co2`, at a given pH and citric/citrate ratio
improves the % / (see Fig. 4).
The e†ect of pH on % / of cobalt electroplating from
bath 10 (containing a constant citric/citrate ratio) is
given in Table 2. The pH was changed by NaOH addi-
tion. The data show that the current efficiency (% /) for
Co deposition increases as the pH of the bath is
increased from pH 3É5 to pH 6É0. The low values of %
/ for Co deposition at low pH values could be due to
the decrease in hydrogen overpotential and consequent-
ly the enhancement of hydrogen evolution reaction,
although lowering the pH increases the concentration of
The e†ect of temperature on % / of cobalt electro-
plating from bath 10 is given in Table 3. A rise in tem-
perature from 25 to 35¡C decreases the % / from
88É7% to 76É4% and a further increase in temperature
has no marked inÑuence on % /. One of the most
important operating conditions is the applied current
density. From Table 4 it can be seen that increasing the

From the above results it is found that the optimum
conditions for producing sound and satisfactory Co
citrate (0É19 mol dm~3) and citric acid (0É1 mol dm~3)
(bath 10), at pH 5É0, i \ 2 A dm~2, 25¡C.
Throwing power and throwing index of the citrate bath
The throwing power (TP) of the optimum bath (10) was
measured using a HaringÈBlum cell under variable
operating conditions such as current density, pH and
temperature. The TP values of these baths computed by
using the empirical FieldÏs formula at a distance ratio of
(1 : 5) are shown in Tables 2È4. Under the chosen
T \ 25¡C for bath 10, it was found that the TP of
citrate baths is greater than that reported from pure sul-
phate baths.11 Inspection of data in Table 4 reveals that
increasing the current density strongly enhances the TP.
This could be attributed to the fact that increasing the
current density results in an increase in cathodic polar-
ization and consequently this is reÑected in an increase
Decreasing the pH value of bath 10 produces poor
TP as shown in Table 2. This could be attributed to the
decrease in cathodic polarization with decreasing pH.
However, increasing the temperature (Table 3) leads to
a slight decrease in TP. This is due to the decrease of
increasing temperature enhances the conductivity of the
Some linear plots between the metal distribution
ratio M, and the linear ratio L (1 : 1È1 : 5) are given in
Fig. 5. The reciprocal of the slope of this plot is called
the throwing index and represents a direct measure of
bath throwing power.20 The values of TI are given in
Tables 2È4. Expressing the results in the form of TI
rather than TP is advantageous because several experi-
mental points are taken during the measurements of the
TI and this minimizes errors in measurement of any one

Surface morphology and structure of the deposits
The cobalt deposits obtained from these baths were
adherent, smooth and bright in appearance. It is worth-
while to mention here that at temperatures higher than
50¡C, the deposits tend to be black and dull.
X-ray di†raction analyses (XRD) were conducted on
i \ 2 A dm~2, pH 5É0 and t \ 20 min (Table 5). The
XRD patterns show that the as-plated cobalt consists of

obtained from bath 10 at deposition potential \ [ 900 mV
and di†erent deposition times (50 s curve 1, 100 s curve 2,
200 s curve 3, 300 s curve 4, 400 s curve 5 and 600 s curve 6,
a mixture of two phases, a-cobalt (hexagonal) and b-
cobalt (face-centred cube). By careful investigation of
the XRD pattern, one can conclude that the crystalline
plate consists of a predominant phase of a-cobalt and a
Typical anodic stripping voltammograms (ASV) for
Co deposition from the optimum bath (10) (pH 5 at
25¡C) are shown in Fig. 6. Each anodic scan exhibits
corresponding to two phases in
the deposit. Taken in consideration with the data of
XRD, it is probable that the more negative anodic peak
is due to the dissolution of Co from the a-phase
could be ascribed to the disso-
lution of Co from the b-phase. It is seen that the two

in the plating time. The time dependence of the poten-
tial in the stripping experiment allows a quantitative

of Bath Constituent, pH and Current Density (time \ 60 min,
analysis of the chemical composition of the deposit and
provides information on the various crystalline phases
The surface morphology of the as-plated cobalt
deposit from bath 10 under di†erent operating condi-
tions was examined by scanning electron microscopy
(SEM) and the results are shown in Fig. 7. The cobalt
electrodeposit at i \ 2 A dm~2, pH \ 5É0, 25¡C and
plating time 20 min, consisted mainly of a disperse, Ðne-
grained deposit covering almost all of the cathode
surface (Fig. 7(a)). At low pH values (Fig. 7(b)) a pitted
dendrite-shaped cobalt plate was deposited. This could
be related to H` ions discharging at the cathode, thus
producing hydrogen gas bubbles which cling to the
growing cobalt deposit. Although the cobalt deposited
at low pH is the most bright, it cannot be recommended
that a bath of low pH (pH \ 4É0) is used because of
vigorous hydrogen evolution at the plate during the
Increasing the applied current density from 1 (Fig.
7(c)) to 2 A dm~2 (Fig. 7(a)) has a slight increasing e†ect
on the rate of the crystal nucleus generation. On the
other hand, it was observed that the surface morphol-
ogy of the deposits varied from a Ðne grain structure to
a large grain structure as the temperature of the bath
was increased. It is concluded that the increasing tem-
perature of the bath accelerated the di†usion rate of the
ion and depressed the adsorption of hydrogen gas on
the deposits. Furthermore, the decrease in cathodic
polarization associated with the increase in temperature
promoted the growth of crystal grains.27
The inÑuence of the bath constituents and some of the
(kgf mm~2) of the as-plated cobalt electroplates were
investigated and the results are given in Table 6.
According to these data, the microhardness of cobalt
electrodeposited from citrate baths is high and it may,
under certain conditions, be two or three times higher
than that reported for cobalt deposited from sulphate,
chloride, bromide and acetate baths.28 This behaviour
could be explained by the inclusion of foreign species
(hydroxide), in agreement with the decrease observed
Table 6 shows that lowering the pH of the cobalt
bath from pH 5É0 (bath 10) to pH 3É5 (bath 8) results in
a decrease in microhardness. This behaviour could be
attributed to the observed decrease in cathodic polar-
ization and an increase in hydrogen evolution which
was accompanied by embrittlement of the electro-
deposits. On the other hand, increasing current density
(from 1 to 2 A dm~2), increases greatly the micro-
hardness from 428 to 1162 kgf mm~2. This is because
increasing the current density enhances the cathodic
Electroplating of cobalt from aqueous citrate baths
Fig. 7. Photomicrographs of cobalt deposited at di†erent plating conditions: (a) cobalt deposited from bath 10, X \ 975
(time \ 20 min, i \ 2 A dm~2, pH \ 5É0); (b) cobalt deposited from bath 8, X \ 975 (time \ 20 min, i \ 2 A dm~2); (c) cobalt
deposited from bath 10, X \ 975 (time \ 20 min, i \ 1 A dm~2, pH \ 5É0); (d) cobalt deposited from bath 10, X \ 65
(time \ 20 min, i \ 2 A dm~2, pH \ 5É0, T \ 50¡C).
polarization and the consequent slight decrease in the
grain size of the cobalt electrodeposit.
Sound, adherent and bright Co deposits were suc-
cessfully obtained from aqueous complex citrate baths.
The optimum plating conditions are: CoSO4 . 7H2O
(0É36 mol dm~3), trisodium citrate (0É19 mol dm~3) and
citric acid (0É1 mol dm~3) at pH 5É0, i \ 2 A dm~2,
t \ 20 min at 25¡C. It was found that the presence of
citrate ions in the solutions enhances greatly the
cathodic polarization due to complexation of Co2`
ions. Increasing the pH of the citrate bath enhances the
cathodic polarization while increasing temperature has
an opposite e†ect on the cathodic polarization. Under
the optimum bath conditions, the cathodic current effi-
ciency, % /, approaches 88É7% and the baths are char-
acterized by a moderate throwing power (36É1%). The
microhardness of Co electrodeposited from these citrate
baths is high. The examination of the surface morphol-
ogy using SEM of the as-plated Co deposit under the
optimum conditions showed that the deposit consisted
of a disperse Ðne grain structure and it changes to a
large grain structure with increasing temperature. X-ray
di†raction analysis (XRD) and anodic stripping voltam-
metry (ASV) proved that the Co deposit under the
optimum conditions consists of a mixture of two phases,
